column4: Post-Gaddafi Libya collapse flooded the Sahel with weapons. Tuareg rebellion + jihadist infiltration in northern Mali. French intervention (2013-2022) failed to stabilize. Military coups in all three countries (2020-2023) expelled French/Western forces.cnn+2
column5: Military juntas (Alliance of Sahel States / AES) + Russian Africa Corps vs. JNIM (al-Qaeda affiliate), Islamic State Sahel Province (ISSP), Tuareg separatists (FLA/Azawad).
column6: JNIM closer to Bamako than at any point in the conflict. AES deployed 15,000 joint troops with Niger/Burkina Faso conducting airstrikes inside Mali. Russian forces increasingly targeted. Western withdrawal created security vacuum. Jihadist capabilities growing (drones, IEDs). Civilian atrocities by all sides.hrw+2
column7: The Sahel is losing ground to jihadists, and the Russian replacement for Western forces has proven ineffective. The AES juntas traded French/UN presence for Russian mercenaries who are less capable and equally predatory toward civilians. JNIM is functionally an insurgent government across large swaths of rural Mali and Burkina Faso, taxing populations and providing rough justice. The 15,000-troop AES deployment is a desperate measure that reveals how badly things have deteriorated. Most likely outcome: jihadist groups consolidate rural control while juntas hold capitals and key cities. This becomes a permanent, low-grade war that destabilizes coastal West Africa (Ghana, Togo, Benin, Ivory Coast are next in the expansion path). No external actor has the will or capability to reverse this trend.cnn+2

column4: Rooted in the 1994 Rwandan genocide aftermath, resource competition, and weak Congolese state control over eastern provinces. Rwanda has backed M23 as a proxy to control mineral-rich Kivu provinces for decades.thesoufancenter+1
column5: DRC Armed Forces (FARDC) + allied militias + MONUSCO vs. M23 (Rwanda-backed), ADF/ISIS-DRC, 100+ other armed groups. Rwanda (Kagame) is the primary external driver.
column6: M23 took Goma (provincial capital) in early 2025--a major escalation. Rwanda effectively annexed parts of North and South Kivu. Doha Framework for Peace signed Nov 2025 but fighting continued. Washington Accords in progress. 5.7 million internally displaced. ADF/ISIS attacks increasing. MONUSCO withdrawing.thesoufancenter+2
column7: Rwanda has achieved its strategic objectives by proxy and will not give them up voluntarily. The Doha and Washington diplomatic tracks are producing paper agreements while Kagame consolidates control over eastern Congo's mineral wealth (coltan, cobalt, gold). The DRC military is too weak and corrupt to retake the territory. MONUSCO's withdrawal removes the last neutral presence. Most likely outcome: a frozen occupation dressed up as a peace process, similar to Russia's approach in Donbas pre-2022. The humanitarian crisis (6.6 million displaced) will persist as a permanent feature. The international community lacks leverage over Rwanda--Kagame is too strategically useful to Western powers to be seriously pressured.thesoufancenter+2

column4: State collapse in 1991 after Siad Barre's overthrow. Al-Shabab emerged from the Islamic Courts Union. AU peacekeeping mission (AMISOM/ATMIS) has propped up the federal government for nearly two decades.cfr+1
column5: Federal Government of Somalia + AU Transition Mission (ATMIS/AUSSOM) + US strikes + Ethiopian/UAE strikes vs. Al-Shabab (al-Qaeda affiliate), ISIS-Somalia (Puntland). Internal: federal-state tensions (Jubaland, Puntland/Somaliland).
column6: Al-Shabab regained territory lost in 2022 government offensive, especially in central Somalia. US/UAE escalated strikes in Puntland against ISIS-Somalia. ATMIS withdrawing troops. 4.4 million facing food insecurity. Journalists and aid workers routinely targeted. Ethiopia-Somalia tensions de-escalated via Turkish mediation.cfryoutube
column7: Al-Shabab is not going away, and the withdrawal of AU troops will create new vacuums. The 2022 government offensive was a temporary success fueled by clan mobilization that has since dissipated. Al-Shabab remains the most capable insurgent organization in Africa with a sophisticated tax/governance system across rural areas. The phased ATMIS withdrawal leaves Somali forces that are riddled with clan loyalty problems and poorly equipped to hold territory. US airstrikes kill individual leaders but do not address the structural drivers. Most likely outcome: continued stalemate with Al-Shabab controlling rural areas indefinitely while the government holds Mogadishu and a few cities. Somalia will not be a functioning unified state in any foreseeable timeframe.cfryoutube

column3: Gang crisis escalation: 2021+; Current emergency: Feb 2024
column4: Decades of political dysfunction, the assassination of President Moise (July 2021), and the vacuum left by the disbanded military. Gangs filled the governance void. Rooted in deep poverty, foreign intervention history (US, UN), and institutional collapse.hrw+1
column5: Viv Ansanm gang coalition (controls most of Port-au-Prince) vs. Haitian National Police + UN-authorized Gang Suppression Force (Kenya-led, transitioned from MSS mission Sept 2025).
column6: Gangs control most of Port-au-Prince and have expanded into 3 of 10 departments. 4,500+ killed by political violence. 1.4 million internally displaced. 5.7 million (half the population) in humanitarian crisis. Gang Suppression Force authorized but under-resourced. 270,000+ Haitians forcibly returned in 2025.hrw+2
column7: Haiti is a failed state in all but name, and the international response is woefully inadequate. The Kenya-led force is too small and too poorly funded to retake Port-au-Prince from entrenched, well-armed gangs. The "Viv Ansanm" coalition controls critical infrastructure including ports and roads. There is no functioning government with democratic legitimacy. The US deportation of Haitians back into this chaos is pouring fuel on the fire. Most likely outcome: gangs remain de facto rulers of urban Haiti for years, with the international force confined to a few secure zones. Without a massive, sustained international military and development commitment (which no country is willing to make), Haiti will continue its descent.hrw+2

column4: Structural: weak institutions, US drug demand, weapons flow from US, corruption at every level. The war has killed 400,000+ people since 2006. Cartels employ ~175,000 people (5th largest employer in Mexico).reliefweb+1
column5: Mexican Armed Forces + law enforcement vs. Sinaloa Cartel, CJNG (Jalisco New Generation Cartel), and dozens of smaller organizations. US involvement via DEA/CIA.
column6: El Mencho (CJNG leader, Mexico's most wanted) killed Feb 22, 2026, triggering violence across 20 of 32 states. 4,000 troops deployed to Jalisco/Nayarit. Succession violence within CJNG ongoing. CIA involvement in operations surfaced. ACLED ranks Mexico as one of the three most dangerous places on Earth.hrw+3
column7: Killing kingpins has never worked and will not work now. Every decapitation strike since Guzman's capture has produced fragmentation, succession wars, and more violence--not less. CJNG will splinter into factions that fight each other and the Sinaloa Cartel for territory, producing a spike in civilian casualties. The fundamental drivers--US demand for drugs, US supply of weapons, Mexican institutional corruption, poverty--remain completely unaddressed. The Trump administration's militarized approach (including CIA operations inside Mexico) may produce tactical wins but strategically guarantees continued escalation. Mexico's cartel war is a permanent condition until demand-side and institutional reforms occur, and no political will exists for either.wikipedia+2

column4: Longest-running conflict in the Western Hemisphere. 2016 FARC peace accord ended the main insurgency but spawned dissident factions. ELN never fully demobilized. Drug trafficking, illegal mining, and deforestation fuel armed groups.hrw+2
column5: Colombian Armed Forces vs. ELN, FARC dissidents (EMC, EMBF, CNEB), Gulf Clan / Gaitanist Army. Petro's "Total Peace" policy.
column6: 2025 was the worst humanitarian year in a decade. Displacement doubled (235,619 people). Explosive device casualties up 33%. Drone attacks up 138%. Presidential candidate assassinated. 2026 elections (May 31) are a referendum on Total Peace vs. military hardline approach.hrw+1
column7: Colombia's "Total Peace" is failing, but the alternative is worse. Petro's negotiation-first approach gave armed groups breathing room to consolidate territory and revenue streams. But the right-wing alternative of pure military escalation was tried for decades under Plan Colombia and produced 10 million victims. The 2026 election will likely produce a rightward shift and military escalation that temporarily suppresses visible violence but drives it underground and into more remote regions. Colombia's conflict is structurally permanent because it is driven by cocaine economics that generate billions annually. Until global drug policy changes, Colombia will cycle between negotiation and escalation indefinitely.hrw+1

column2: Yemen (Multi-Party Civil War + Houthi Maritime Campaign)
column3: 2014 (Houthi takeover of Sanaa); Saudi intervention March 2015
column4: Rooted in north-south divisions, Houthi insurgency (Zaydi Shia), Arab Spring (2011), Hadi government collapse. Saudi-led coalition intervened 2015. Houthis became Iranian proxy. Red Sea attacks began Oct 2023 in solidarity with Gaza.nbcnews+1youtube
column5: Houthis (Ansar Allah, Iran-backed) vs. Internationally recognized government (PLC, fractured: Saudi-backed vs. UAE-backed STC). US/UK conducted strikes on Houthi targets.
column6: 22 million need humanitarian aid (half the population). PLC factions fought each other (Dec 2025-Jan 2026) over southeastern governorates. Iran pressuring Houthis to "close" Bab el-Mandeb. Red Sea quieter than 2024 peak but unstable. Houthis detaining 73 UN personnel. 2026 aid appeal only 29% funded.nbcnews+1youtube
column7: Yemen is a conflict nobody is trying to end. The Houthis are the de facto government of northwestern Yemen and will remain so. The "legitimate" government is split between Saudi and UAE-backed factions that fight each other as much as the Houthis. Iran uses the Houthis as a strategic lever (Red Sea disruption capability) and will not abandon them. Saudi Arabia wants out but cannot find an exit that preserves face. Most likely outcome: indefinite fragmentation with three or more de facto statelets, periodic Red Sea escalation tied to broader regional dynamics (Iran war), and a permanent humanitarian catastrophe that the world funds at 30 cents on the dollar. Yemen will not be reunified in any of our lifetimes.nbcnews+1youtube
